definition: Having great physical beauty and appeal.
example: The pulchritudinous valley at sunrise left the tourists standing in stunned silence.
evolution_story: From the Latin 'pulchritudo', meaning beauty. The word existed in Middle English but completely vanished from British usage in the 17th century! It was dramatically revived in American English in the late 19th century, and today it is most often used with deliberate humour or irony, since the word itself sounds wonderfully strange for something that means 'beautiful'.
trivia: This is considered one of the most paradoxical words in the English language: it means 'breathtakingly beautiful', but the word itself — with its hard 'k', 'ch', and 'd' sounds — is widely considered one of the most ugly-sounding words ever created!

word_in_action.source: The Most Beautiful Places in the World, National Geographic, 2024
word_in_action.text: A breathtaking travel feature using elevated language to portray landscapes so stunning they are described as pulchritudinous scenes straight from a dream.

word_in_action.source: Renaissance Art and Beauty, The New York Times, 2023
word_in_action.text: An art review using the word to describe the pulchritudinous figures in Botticelli's paintings, noting that Renaissance artists treated physical beauty as a form of divine perfection.
